text: Hello, I am sharing a case of a colorectal anastomotic stricture. This is a patient who underwent a sigmoid resection with splenic flexion mobilization by another surgeon about two and a half years ago. About three months ago, they presented to the emergency room with a large bowel obstruction and it was determined to be at the level of the anastomosis and a gastroenterologist had done a endoscopic balloon dilation with temporary relief of her symptoms. She then obstructed again, and the second time the gastroenterologist placed a stent. And although her symptoms had dramatically improved, we knew that this wasn't a long-term solution.

Hello, I am sharing a case of a colorectal anastomotic stricture. This is a patient who underwent a sigmoid resection with splenic flexion mobilization by another surgeon about two and a half years ago. About three months ago, they presented to the emergency room with a large bowel obstruction and it was determined to be at the level of the anastomosis and a gastroenterologist had done a endoscopic balloon dilation with temporary relief of her symptoms. She then obstructed again, and the second time the gastroenterologist placed a stent. And although her symptoms had dramatically improved, we knew that this wasn't a long-term solution. She was referred to me for consultation after the stent had been placed. You can see the stent there, and you see a little bit of inflammation going to the dome of the bladder. At the time that I did this operation, it was about two months after the stent had been placed. Again, this is a benign colorectal anastomotic stricture.

In the operating room, the surgeon retracts the colon with their left hand while carefully detaching loops of small bowel from adhesions in the pelvis. The presence of a stent and inflammation near the bladder dome is visible. The surgeon repairs serosal tears on the small bowel loops.

In the operating room, the surgeon retracts the colon with their left hand while carefully detaching loops of small bowel tethered in the pelvis due to adhesions. The surgeon repairs serosal tears on the small bowel, visible from previous stitches.

The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions involving the small bowel, ensuring no injury occurs. The fused sigmoid mesentery is visible, attached to the retroperitoneum over the sacral promontory. The surgeon begins marking the area for resection.

The surgeon addresses the fused sigmoid mesentery to the retroperitoneum over the sacral promontory, marking the area for resection. The dissection proceeds laterally, revealing the superior aspect of the stent and dense adherence between the colon, stent, and bladder. Ureteral catheters and ICG are used to ensure the ureter and bladder are not compromised. The surgeon identifies wires of the stent, indicating possible necrosis or perforation into the bladder, and continues to dissect below the inflammatory reaction to prepare for a new anastomosis.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection, pressing on the superior aspect of the stent and proceeding towards the dome of the bladder. Dense adhesions are visible between the colon, stent, and bladder, resembling a colovesical fistula. Ureteral catheters and ICG are used to ensure the ureter is not nearby and the bladder remains intact. The wires of the stent are visible, indicating possible necrosis or perforation into the bladder.

The surgeon navigates below the inflammatory reaction to create a new wide open anastomosis, carefully dissecting down the right side. The stent is visible as it has eroded through, and the surgeon seeks a plane above the colon wall, avoiding perforation of the bladder.

The surgeon navigates dense fibrotic adherence between the colon and bladder, using robotic instruments to find a proper dissection plane. The stent is visible, having eroded through the colon wall, as the surgeon carefully separates the colon from the bladder without causing perforation.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to navigate dense fibrotic adhesions, working to separate the colon from the bladder. The colon is carefully dissected anteriorly off the bladder wall, revealing the normal rectum beneath and ensuring the dissection is below the level of the stent. The surgeon identifies the mesorectum and locates the proper plane for resection.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, focusing on the interior wall of the colon and aiming to reach the normal rectum below the stent level. The mesorectum is identified, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the mesentery to the mid rectum, ensuring clearance below the stent. The splenic flexure is remobilized to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon clears the mesentery to the mid rectum, ensuring a tension-free anastomosis by remobilizing the splenic flexure. The mesentery is taken up to the bowel wall to prepare a surface for stapling, avoiding bleeding. The anterior wall of the rectum is opened to visualize the distal extent of the stent, preventing stapling across it. The distal aspect of the stent is visible, and the surgeon staples across, requiring an additional staple to complete the division. The proximal margin is cleaned up, located just proximal to the stent.

The surgeon carefully approaches the bowel wall to prepare a surface for the stapler, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The anterior wall of the rectum is opened to visually confirm the distal extent of the stent, avoiding stapling across it. The distal aspect of the stent is visible, and the surgeon uses a stapler to divide the tissue, requiring an additional staple to complete the division. The proximal margin is cleaned up, located just proximal to the stent, and is easier to feel, allowing division without opening this part of the bowel.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis by joining two staple lines using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, gently bringing the two ends together. The patient remains in steep Trendelenburg position to prevent the small bowel from filling the pelvis, ensuring a tension-free anastomosis despite the challenging remobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis by joining the two staple lines with a 3-0 V-Loc suture. The patient is kept in steep Trendelenburg position to manage the small bowel, which fills the pelvis when repositioned. The surgeon uses three robotic arms, with one arm holding the small bowel out of the way. The posterior inner row is created by adjoining the lip between the otomy and the staple lines. The surgeon checks the tension and begins the second inner suture, ensuring precise stitch placement.

The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis, joining the posterior staple lines and creating a proctotomy. The small bowel is held in the right lower quadrant with sponges for clear exposure. Using three robotic arms, the surgeon completes the inner layer by sewing the posterior inner row, ensuring precise stitch placement. The procedure involves alternating between left and right hands to form the inner anterior row with small bites of seromuscular tissue, occasionally including mucosa.

The surgeon sews around the near corner, taking large bites and switching to the left hand to form the inner anterior row with small bites of seromuscular tissue, occasionally catching some mucosa. The stitches are tied together to complete the anterior row of a two-layer anastomosis. The original suture is then used to imbricate over the top, completing the full two-layer anastomosis.

The surgeon ties the stitches together to complete a two-layer anastomosis, using an imbricating lumbar suture over the top of the staple lines. The mucosa is caught for hemostasis, but does not add strength to the anastomosis.
